ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for packaged prune juice; SNOMED CT coverage for specific fruit juices is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of packaged prune juice is one glass of approximately 240 ml (about 240 g), commonly consumed as a morning beverage or digestive aid.

nutrient_source.title: Prune juice, canned — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 09209)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary nutrient values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, dietary fibre, sodium, calcium, iron) are sourced from Singapore HPB Foodo database (D03040024) with confidence 0.95. Additional micronutrients (potassium, saturated fat, vitamin C, vitamin B6, vitamin B3, phosphorus, magnesium) are cross-referenced and supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 09209 (prune juice, canned), which is the closest approved-source equivalent for packaged prune juice. USDA values used only for fields not covered by HPB.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Prune juice GI of 43 (±3) is reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and the 2008 International Tables of GI and GL values (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008). This is an exact match for prune juice. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI × carbohydrate per serving (g) / 100 = 43 × 42.72 / 100 = 18.4, rounded to 18.5 for a 240 g serving (17.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 2.4 = 42.72 g carbohydrate per serving).

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (13.84 g/100 g) in a beverage exceeds the 5 g/100 g beverage sugar threshold; liquid sugar drives insulin-mediated sodium retention and independently elevates blood pressure risk.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit packaged prune juice to no more than 100 ml per occasion due to its high sugar content (13.84 g/100 g), which independently raises blood pressure risk via insulin-mediated sodium retention, despite its low sodium (15 mg/100 g) and beneficial potassium (276 mg/100 g); whole prunes are a preferable alternative as they retain fibre that moderates sugar absorption.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Fruit juice classified as sugar-sweetened beverage for diabetes management; sugar 13.84 g/100 g with minimal fibre (0.79 g/100 g) results in rapid glucose absorption without fibre modulation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Packaged prune juice should be avoided or limited to a maximum of 100 ml per occasion for individuals with type 2 diabetes, as it is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage with 13.84 g sugar/100 g and only 0.79 g dietary fibre/100 g, resulting in rapid postprandial glucose elevation; whole prunes (GI ~29) are strongly preferred over juice as they retain fibre that substantially moderates glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sugar content (13.84 g/100 g) in liquid form raises cardiovascular risk via triglyceride elevation and insulin resistance; partially offset by meaningful potassium (276 mg/100 g), negligible saturated fat (0.01 g/100 g), and polyphenol content (chlorogenic acid).
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, packaged prune juice should be consumed in small portions only (100–150 ml maximum) due to its high sugar content (13.84 g/100 g), which can elevate triglycerides and promote insulin resistance; its potassium content (276 mg/100 g) and polyphenols (chlorogenic acid) provide modest cardiovascular benefit, but these do not outweigh the risks of regular high-volume consumption.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Packaged prune juice is a ready-to-drink, commercially processed fruit juice made from dried plums (prunes), typically sold in cartons or bottles. It is produced by extracting juice from rehydrated or cooked prunes, resulting in a dark reddish-brown liquid with a sweet, mildly tart, and slightly earthy flavour. Unlike whole prunes, packaged prune juice contains very little dietary fibre (0.79 g/100 g versus approximately 7.1 g/100 g in whole prunes), as the juicing process removes most of the pulp and skin. It provides 74 kcal per 100 g, with carbohydrates as the dominant macronutrient (17.8 g/100 g), of which 13.84 g/100 g is sugar. Fat content is negligible (0.05 g/100 g). Prune juice is a notable source of potassium (276 mg/100 g), which supports cardiovascular and muscular function, and contains modest amounts of iron (0.77 mg/100 g), calcium (14 mg/100 g), vitamin B6 (0.22 mg/100 g), and vitamin C (4.5 mg/100 g). It retains polyphenols from prunes, particularly chlorogenic acid, which have antioxidant and mild anti-inflammatory properties. Prune juice is widely consumed for its laxative and digestive health benefits, attributed to its sorbitol content and mild fibre. Clinically, it is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage for diabetes and hypertension management due to its high sugar content and low fibre, making it unsuitable for regular consumption by individuals with type 2 diabetes or hypertension. Its glycemic index is 43 (low), but the glycemic load per standard 240 ml serving is approximately 18.5 (medium-high), reflecting the large carbohydrate load per serving. Whole prunes are consistently preferred over juice for individuals managing blood glucose or cardiovascular risk.

Is prune juice good for iron deficiency?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, dietary fibre, sodium, calcium, iron) are sourced directly from the Singapore HPB Foodo database with a confidence of 0.95 for an exact match (D03040024). Additional micronutrients are cross-referenced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 09209 (prune juice, canned), an approved secondary source. GI value of 43 is from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008 (peer-reviewed, exact match). Ontology codes are defensible. No invented values.
notes: 1. Primary nutrient values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, dietary fibre, sodium, calcium, iron) sourced from Singapore HPB Foodo database (record D03040024, confidence 0.95). Additional micronutrients supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 09209 (prune juice, canned) as the closest approved-source equivalent. 2. trans_fat_g is set to 0.0 (confirmed zero — negligible trans fat in fruit juice per USDA SR Legacy). cholesterol_mg is set to 0.0 (confirmed zero — plant-derived beverage contains no cholesterol). vitamin_a_mcg_rae is set to 0.0 (confirmed negligible per USDA SR Legacy). vitamin_b12_mcg is set to 0.0 (confirmed zero — plant-derived food). vitamin_d_mcg is set to 0.0 (confirmed zero — not a significant source per USDA SR Legacy). 3. Potassium value of 276 mg/100 g is consistent with both the input record's health_suitability notes and USDA SR Legacy (FDC 09209 reports 276 mg/100 g). 4. GI of 43 is from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 International Tables (Diabetes Care). Glycemic load per 240 g serving = 43 × (17.8 × 2.4) / 100 = 43 × 42.72 / 100 ≈ 18.4, reported as 18.5. 5. This is a globally distributed packaged beverage with no specific Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese cuisine classification; therefore, no mandatory local-language names apply under G6 multilingual rules. Local names fields are set to null. 6. The rheumatoid_arthritis health suitability entry in the input contained an internal contradiction (the reason stated polyphenols qualify as positive anti-inflammatory factors, but the cautions stated no positive anti-inflammatory factor is present). This contradiction has been resolved in the health_context section by providing consistent, evidence-based clinical guidance for the three mandatory conditions (hypertension, type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular disease). 7. FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03301710 and FoodEx2 code A00LC are used as defensible ontology anchors for prune juice; these are the best available matches in the respective ontologies.
